---
title: "White Wine Quality Analysis"
author: "Justin Kim, Aryan Arora, Shuri Yamamoto and Erhan Asad Javed"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Summary

This study explored the physicochemical characteristics of white wines and their relationship with quality ratings using exploratory data analysis and machine learning. The analysis showed that most wines fall within mid-range quality scores, with relatively few very low or very high ratings. Several chemical variables exhibit skewed distributions and occasional extreme values, while correlations suggest that wine quality is influenced by multiple factors rather than a single dominant variable. A random forest model was used to predict wine quality, achieving moderate accuracy but showing lower performance on minority classes due to class imbalance. These findings highlight the complexity of predicting wine quality and suggest that future work could improve results by addressing class imbalance and exploring more advanced models.

# Introduction

The goal of this project is to analyze and predict white wine quality based on physicochemical properties such as alcohol content, acidity, and sugar levels. Understanding the factors that influence wine quality can help producers improve production processes and ensure consistent product quality.

Previous research has demonstrated that data mining and machine learning techniques can effectively model wine quality using physicochemical attributes @cortez2009. Additionally, ensemble learning approaches have been shown to improve predictive performance in similar classification problems @sagi2018.

This analysis aims to identify key variables that impact wine quality and evaluate how well these variables can be used to predict quality outcomes.

## Data Description

The dataset used in this analysis contains measurements of various physicochemical properties of white wine samples. These include variables such as alcohol content, pH, density, residual sugar, and other chemical characteristics.

The target variable is wine quality, which is measured on a numerical scale. This dataset enables the exploration of relationships between these features and wine quality, forming the basis for predictive modeling.

# Methods

To analyze the relationship between physicochemical properties and wine quality, a predictive modeling approach was used. The dataset was cleaned and preprocessed to ensure consistency and accuracy.

Machine learning techniques are commonly applied in predictive modeling tasks due to their ability to capture complex relationships between variables @rodriguez2015. In this analysis, a random forest classifier was used to identify key predictors of wine quality and evaluate predictive performance.

Model performance was assessed using relevant evaluation metrics to determine how accurately wine quality could be predicted.

# Exploratory Data Analysis

![](../output/eda/wine_quality_histogram.png){#fig-quality width=70%}

 @fig-quality shows the distribution of wine quality scores. Most wines fall within mid-range values, particularly between scores of 5 and 7, indicating a class imbalance with fewer extreme quality ratings.

![](../output/eda/data_split_pie_chart.png){#fig-split width=70%}

 @fig-split illustrates the proportion of data allocated to training, validation, and test sets, ensuring proper evaluation of model performance.

![](../output/eda/feature_distributions.png){#fig-features width=70%}

 @fig-features shows the distribution of key physicochemical variables. Many features exhibit skewness and varying ranges, with some variables displaying extreme values.

![](../output/eda/correlation_matrix.png){#fig-corr width=70%}

 @fig-corr displays correlations between variables. Most features show weak relationships with wine quality, suggesting that quality depends on multiple interacting factors rather than a single dominant variable.

# Results

![](../output/prediction/confusion_matrix.png){#fig-conf width=70%}

 @fig-conf shows the performance of the random forest model on test data. The confusion matrix indicates moderate predictive performance, with stronger accuracy for mid-range classes and weaker performance for less frequent classes.

The model achieved moderate accuracy, but performance varied across classes due to class imbalance. Metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score indicate that the model struggles to correctly classify minority classes.

# Discussion

The exploratory data analysis suggests that most wines in the dataset fall into average quality categories, with ratings concentrated around the middle of the scale. This reflects class imbalance, where extremely high or low quality wines are relatively uncommon.

The distributions of physicochemical variables indicate that most values lie within typical ranges, although some variables such as residual sugar and chlorides exhibit wider spreads and occasional extreme values. Several features are right-skewed, indicating that most wines have lower values with a few higher observations.

The correlation analysis shows that no single variable strongly determines wine quality. Instead, quality is influenced by a combination of multiple chemical characteristics.

The random forest model achieved moderate predictive performance, with reduced accuracy for minority classes. This limitation is largely due to class imbalance and the complexity of the prediction task.

Future work could address class imbalance using techniques such as oversampling or class weighting. Additionally, more advanced models such as gradient boosting or neural networks may improve predictive performance. Incorporating additional data sources may also enhance model accuracy.

# References
